category|Category:Narumi Hachiman-gū}} [[JA:鳴海八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11675363}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Narumi Hachimangu Shrine |Image = [[File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 01.jpg|250px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Midori Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155209|Midori Ward}}{{jalink|Narumi Town (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Narumi Town}} Aza Maenowa 23 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|35|4|28|N|136|56|36|E|}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Emperor Ojin}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803|Empress Jingu}}
{{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyuga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayori Bisho Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Niyōbi Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukiyomi Mikoto}} |Shakaku = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}Ronsha
{{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Regular festival = Saturday in the first half of October |Alternative name = Hachiman
Narumi Shrine
Bettemiya
Kawahara Shrine |Foundation = unknown (before {{jalink|13th century|Q7049}})|Map = {{Location map|Nagoya|lat_deg=35|lat_min=4|lat_sec=28.1|lon_deg=136|lon_min=56|lon_sec=36.3|label=Narumi Hachimangu Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|4|28.1|N|136|56|36.3|E}}}} }}'''Narumi Hachiman Shrine'' (Narumi Hachimangu) is {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Midori Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155209|Midori Ward}}{{jalink|Narumi Town {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=8}} located in (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Narumi Town}}. {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}{{jalink|Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|Aichi District}} It is also said to be the Ronsha of ``{{jalink|Kawahara Shrine|Q3194327}}'' and the ronsha of ``Izoe Shrine.'' === Overview === It has been called "Hachiman" since ancient times, and was considered to be a separate shrine to {{jalink|Narumi Shrine|Q11495515}}. The location of the shrine was {{jalink|Edago|Q11533553}} in {{jalink|Narumi Village}}, in the area that was called Zennoan Village in the {{jalink|Edo period}}. The year of its founding is unknown, but there are records of shrine occupations dating back to the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, and a {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}} that is over 1,000 years old remains within the precincts, suggesting that it may be an old shrine that dates back to before the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. As mentioned later, it is Shikinaisha's Ronsha. Around this shrine, the remains of a village from the {{jalink|Yayoi period|Q207858}}, ``Maenowa Ruins,'' have been discovered, and {{jalink|Yayoi pottery|Q11487461}} have been unearthed. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} (Shinada Wake no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}} (Senagatai Hibi no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|Tamayoribibimei}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Nishi Geishi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukiyomi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} === History === The company seems to have already existed in the Kamakura period, and there is a record that Motohisa Oga, the first generation member of the Kuno family, became a company official in the first year of {{jalink|Sadanaga|Q1194168}} ({{jalink|1232|Q5418}}). Since ancient times, it has been called ``Hachimangu Shrine'' and was treated as a companion to Narumi Togu Shrine (Narumi Shrine). {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|During the Warring States period}}, there is a record that {{jalink|Yoshimoto Imagawa|Q1054305}} and {{jalink|Shigemasa Yamaguchi|Q11467507|Chōjiro Shigemasa}} {{jalink|corporate territory}} was {{jalink|donated|Q11455717}} to our company and Narumi Togu Shrine. According to ``{{jalink|Owari Makiyōki}},'' the priest Mr. Kuno submitted a document stating that Narumi Hachimangu Shrine is ``Kawahara Shrine,'' Aichi District, Owari Province, in ``{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}.'' According to ``{{jalink|Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092}}'', there are some Edo-era fishing lanterns and {{jalink|ridge tags|Q8008834}} that have the words ``Kawahara Shrine'' written on them.On the other hand, there is a theory that it corresponds to "Izoe Shrine" in Aichi District, Owari Province, but according to Kunihiko Sakakibara, a {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Efn|{{jalink|local historian}}, Izoe Shrine was built during the Heian period. When it was abolished in the late period, Atsuta Daiguji disowned Hachimangu Shrine, and during the Oei era ({{jalink|1394|Q6398}} to {{jalink|1428|Q6315}}), the enshrinement site of Izoe Shrine, which had been abolished, was renamed to the old Jizoyama ({{jalink|Narumi Town) (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Q136913130|Q136913130|Komatsuyama, Narumi Town), it is said to have been rebuilt as a Hachimangu Shrine in the former Tange (Kiyomizu Temple, Narumi Town). It is said that in the early Muromachi period, it was moved from that place to the present-day Azazenowa area, which was at the mouth of the {{jalink|Ogigawa|Q11496839|Kurosuegawa}} large camphor tree. n|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|1984|pp=103}}. Izoe Shrine was later enshrined as Kitanosha, a subsidiary shrine of Hachimangu Shrine, but it is said to have been abolished in the Edo period {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}. }}, from the description in ``{{jalink|Owari Province Fudoki|Q135598023}}'', it is presumed that Izoe Shrine was located in Aichi District {{jalink|Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture) #Go|Q552687|Hibego}}, and it is unreasonable to compare our company with Izoe Shrine.In {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}3 ({{jalink|1690|Q7678}}), he served as a priest at Narumi Shrine along with the Makino family.{{jalink| Narumi-juku|Q3033344}}The Kuno family of Honmachi moved to Maenowa and served as a priest.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink|1700|Q6813}}), the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests quarreled and the festival was divided. The parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In recent years, efforts have been seen to bring together the front and back sides of the festival and gather floats at Narumi-juku Honmachi.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} === Chronology === * {{jalink|Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|Koji}} December 3, 17 ({{jalink|1557|Q6595}}) - Yoshimoto Imagawa donates Kanda 11 kan 50 mon to Hachiman Shrine and Narumi Togu (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 17th year ({{jalink|1589|Q6759}}) - Chojiro Shigemasa Yamaguchi donated 20 kanmon of shrine territory to the priests of both Hachimangu and Narumi Togu shrines.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{jalink|Genna (Japan)|Q635245|Genna}} August 8 ({{jalink|1622|Q6815}}) - Reconstruction of the main shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink|1700|Q6813}}) - As a result of an argument between the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests, the parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. * {{jalink|Culture|Q11042}} 1st year ({{jalink|1804|Q6894}}) - Constructed a water fountain using trees in the temple grounds {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}}2nd year ({{jalink|1819|Q6967}}) - Rebuild the Chozuya.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}.* Bunsei 12 ({{jalink|1819|Q6967}}) - Rebuild the worship hall, watariden, etc.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}18 ({{jalink|1733|Q6972}}) November 1st - {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} Lord {{jalink|Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachimangu Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}. * August 19, 4th year of {{jalink|Yasunaga}} ({{jalink|1775|Q7700}}) - {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} lord {{jalink|Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachiman Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=34}}. * {{jalink|Original|Q1145754}}2nd year ({{jalink|1737|Q6993}}) - Otabisho was built on the banks of the Ogi River{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}}6 ({{jalink|1859|Q7687}}) - Torii was rebuilt{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 28, {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}) - Promoted to {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Village company}}. The name of the shrine was changed from Hachiman-sha to Hachiman-gu {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}.* Meiji 13 ({{jalink|1880|Q7806}}) - Construction of the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. *October 26, 1907 ({{jalink|1907|Q2048}}) - Became a village shrine designated by Heihakuryokushinsha {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}6 ({{jalink|1931|Q18782}}) - Constructing the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * April 1934 ({{jalink|1934|Q18714}}) - Built a new inn on the banks of the Ogi River.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 1956 ({{jalink|1956|Q5221}}) - The former {{jalink|Narumi Town|Q11675375}} government building was relocated as a company office {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * 1969 ({{jalink|1959|Q5302}}) - Damaged by {{jalink|Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156}}, including the worship hall and water purification building.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * 1968 ({{jalink|1968|Q2644}}) - The worship hall, main hall, subsidiary shrine, and chozusha were newly built, and the name was changed to Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. * 1973 ({{jalink|1973|Q2477}}) - Transferred Suesha Inarisha from Aiden to Betsuden.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}* {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}12 ({{jalink|2000|Q1985}}) - Transferred the subsidiary shrine Kitano Tenmansha from the side hall to the separate hall{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}} === Main annual events === * Saitan Festival: New Year's Day {{jalink|New Year's Day|Q196627|New Year's Day}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Kitano Tenmansha {{jalink|Regular Festival|Q11385469}}: Early New Year {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Setsubun|Q746798}} Festival: February Setsubun Day {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Kigen Festival|Q11604982|Kigen Festival}}: February 11th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|prayer festival|Q118906710}}: Sunday in the second half of March {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Inari God|Q719665|Inari}} Festival: Sunday in May {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Ring ritual: July 30th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Korazusha Annual Festival: August 12th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Reitaisai (Omote Festival): Saturday in the first half of October {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387}}: Sunday in the first half of November {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Ninamesai|Q11501518}}: Around November 23rd{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Akibasha Annual Festival: December 16th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Emperor's Birthday|Q72318|Tencho Festival}}: December 23rd {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}}* New Year's Eve Festival ({{jalink|Great Purification|Q11261910}}): December 31st {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Monthly Festival: First half of every month {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} === Precincts === === Sacred tree === It is a large camphor tree with a trunk 8 meters in diameter.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. It is said that it was already a large tree during the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}, and is estimated to be 1000 to 1200 years old. In 2000 ({{jalink|2000|Q1985}}), the Aichi Prefectural Arborist Association conducted treatment{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=23}}. === Office === It was built in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}36 ({{jalink|1903|Q2045}}) as the {{jalink|Narumi Town|Q11675375}} government building. {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}} It was moved to its current location as a shrine office in July of 1931 ({{jalink|1956|Q5221}}). It has been certified as a certified regional building asset by Nagoya City {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}. === Setsusha === Currently, there are the following ten subsidiary companies: {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=11}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}}. In the past, there were about five other companies. {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Current Sessuesha === * Inari Shrine: {{jalink|Uka no Mitama}} Okami{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=11}} * Korasha: {{jalink|Kenuchi Sukune}} Life{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kitano Tenmansha: {{jalink|Michizane Sugawara|Q382005}}Ko{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Akibasha: {{jalink|Kikunshuchi}} Life{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kokuzosha: {{jalink|Okuninushikami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Susanoosha: {{jalink|Susanoo}} Life{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kotohira Shrine: {{jalink|Oimono Nushikami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Okuwa Shrine: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Toyouke Omikami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Korazusha: {{jalink|Chikaji Onna no Mikoto|Q11596349|Chikkana no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Haraido Shrine: {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhime}} God, {{jalink|Kebukito Lord God|Q86734190}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Old Setsuesha === * Kitanosha: {{jalink|Takamisuhigami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kokuzogu: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Doubunsha: {{jalink|Sarutabikojin}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Imori Shrine: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|Dainichikai Takayuki Spirit}}, {{jalink|Susanoo Mikoto||Susanoo Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Isagami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Gallery === File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 02.jpg|Torii File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 04.jpg|Kitano Tenmansha File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 03.jpg|Korasusha === Festival === In the past, the festival was held throughout the town on the same day as {{jalink|Narumi Shrine|Q11495515}}, which is also located in Narumi, but in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink |1700|Q6813}}), a dispute arose between the two shrines, and since then the festival has been divided into front (Narumi Hachiman Shrine) and back (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=34}}. In recent years, there have been efforts to unite the front and back sides and gather floats in Narumi-juku Honmachi, and the festival is now being held as before the festival was divided.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} The Narumi Hachiman Shrine annual festival (Omote Festival) used to be held on August 15th, but since the first year of the Taisho era ({{jalink|1912|Q2077}}) it has been held on {{jalink|September 15th|Q2846}}, and since the Showa era it has been held on {{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}}. In recent years, the annual festival day has been set on a Saturday in the first half of {{jalink|October|Q124}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}. At the Shōjo Festival, a giant doll called {{jalink|shōjō}} made of a bamboo framework with a red face mask and upper half of the body covered with a costume appears, and there is the ``Narumi Hachiman Shrine Reitaisai Festival'' (Omote Festival), where adults wear shōjō dolls and chase children around. Shōto is the deity of Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. The monkeys hit children with fake hands and sticks, and it is said that children who are hit by the monkeys do not get sick.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes walk from {{jalink|Nagoya Railway|Q30850|Meitetsu}}{{jalink|Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|Q1137838|Nagoya Main Line}} {{jalink|Narumi Station|Q6966454}}. * About 20 minutes walk from {{jalink|Tokai Railway Company|Q513679|JR}}{{jalink|Tokaido Line (Nagoya area)|Q11235139|Tokaido Main Line}} {{jalink|Odaka Station|Q871424}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Source === category|Category:Narumi Hachiman-gū}} [[JA:鳴海八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11675363}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Narumi Hachimangu Shrine |Image = [[File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 01.jpg|250px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Midori Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155209|Midori Ward}}{{jalink|Narumi Town (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Narumi Town}} Aza Maenowa 23 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|35|4|28|N|136|56|36|E|}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Emperor Ojin}}
{{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803|Empress Jingu}}
{{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyuga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayori Bisho Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Niyōbi Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukiyomi Mikoto}} |Shakaku = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}Ronsha
{{jalink|Murasha|Q134917284}} |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |Regular festival = Saturday in the first half of October |Alternative name = Hachiman
Narumi Shrine
Bettemiya
Kawahara Shrine |Foundation = unknown (before {{jalink|13th century|Q7049}})|Map = {{Location map|Nagoya|lat_deg=35|lat_min=4|lat_sec=28.1|lon_deg=136|lon_min=56|lon_sec=36.3|label=Narumi Hachimangu Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|4|28.1|N|136|56|36.3|E}}}} }}'''Narumi Hachiman Shrine'' (Narumi Hachimangu) is {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Nagoya City|Q11751}}{{jalink|Midori Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155209|Midori Ward}}{{jalink|Narumi Town {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=8}} located in (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Narumi Town}}. {{jalink|Owari Province|Q514506}}{{jalink|Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|Aichi District}} It is also said to be the Ronsha of ``{{jalink|Kawahara Shrine|Q3194327}}'' and the ronsha of ``Izoe Shrine.'' === Overview === It has been called "Hachiman" since ancient times, and was considered to be a separate shrine to {{jalink|Narumi Shrine|Q11495515}}. The location of the shrine was {{jalink|Edago|Q11533553}} in {{jalink|Narumi Village}}, in the area that was called Zennoan Village in the {{jalink|Edo period}}. The year of its founding is unknown, but there are records of shrine occupations dating back to the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, and a {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}} that is over 1,000 years old remains within the precincts, suggesting that it may be an old shrine that dates back to before the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. As mentioned later, it is Shikinaisha's Ronsha. Around this shrine, the remains of a village from the {{jalink|Yayoi period|Q207858}}, ``Maenowa Ruins,'' have been discovered, and {{jalink|Yayoi pottery|Q11487461}} have been unearthed. === Enshrined God === * {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}} (Shinada Wake no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}} (Senagatai Hibi no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|Tamayoribibimei}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Nishi Geishi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukiyomi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} === History === The company seems to have already existed in the Kamakura period, and there is a record that Motohisa Oga, the first generation member of the Kuno family, became a company official in the first year of {{jalink|Sadanaga|Q1194168}} ({{jalink|1232|Q5418}}). Since ancient times, it has been called ``Hachimangu Shrine'' and was treated as a companion to Narumi Togu Shrine (Narumi Shrine). {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|During the Warring States period}}, there is a record that {{jalink|Yoshimoto Imagawa|Q1054305}} and {{jalink|Shigemasa Yamaguchi|Q11467507|Chōjiro Shigemasa}} {{jalink|corporate territory}} was {{jalink|donated|Q11455717}} to our company and Narumi Togu Shrine. According to ``{{jalink|Owari Makiyōki}},'' the priest Mr. Kuno submitted a document stating that Narumi Hachimangu Shrine is ``Kawahara Shrine,'' Aichi District, Owari Province, in ``{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}.'' According to ``{{jalink|Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092}}'', there are some Edo-era fishing lanterns and {{jalink|ridge tags|Q8008834}} that have the words ``Kawahara Shrine'' written on them.On the other hand, there is a theory that it corresponds to "Izoe Shrine" in Aichi District, Owari Province, but according to Kunihiko Sakakibara, a {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Efn|{{jalink|local historian}}, Izoe Shrine was built during the Heian period. When it was abolished in the late period, Atsuta Daiguji disowned Hachimangu Shrine, and during the Oei era ({{jalink|1394|Q6398}} to {{jalink|1428|Q6315}}), the enshrinement site of Izoe Shrine, which had been abolished, was renamed to the old Jizoyama ({{jalink|Narumi Town) (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Q136913130|Q136913130|Komatsuyama, Narumi Town), it is said to have been rebuilt as a Hachimangu Shrine in the former Tange (Kiyomizu Temple, Narumi Town). It is said that in the early Muromachi period, it was moved from that place to the present-day Azazenowa area, which was at the mouth of the {{jalink|Ogigawa|Q11496839|Kurosuegawa}} large camphor tree. n|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|1984|pp=103}}. Izoe Shrine was later enshrined as Kitanosha, a subsidiary shrine of Hachimangu Shrine, but it is said to have been abolished in the Edo period {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}. }}, from the description in ``{{jalink|Owari Province Fudoki|Q135598023}}'', it is presumed that Izoe Shrine was located in Aichi District {{jalink|Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture) #Go|Q552687|Hibego}}, and it is unreasonable to compare our company with Izoe Shrine.In {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}3 ({{jalink|1690|Q7678}}), he served as a priest at Narumi Shrine along with the Makino family.{{jalink| Narumi-juku|Q3033344}}The Kuno family of Honmachi moved to Maenowa and served as a priest.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink|1700|Q6813}}), the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests quarreled and the festival was divided. The parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In recent years, efforts have been seen to bring together the front and back sides of the festival and gather floats at Narumi-juku Honmachi.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} === Chronology === * {{jalink|Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|Koji}} December 3, 17 ({{jalink|1557|Q6595}}) - Yoshimoto Imagawa donates Kanda 11 kan 50 mon to Hachiman Shrine and Narumi Togu (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}} 17th year ({{jalink|1589|Q6759}}) - Chojiro Shigemasa Yamaguchi donated 20 kanmon of shrine territory to the priests of both Hachimangu and Narumi Togu shrines.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{jalink|Genna (Japan)|Q635245|Genna}} August 8 ({{jalink|1622|Q6815}}) - Reconstruction of the main shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink|1700|Q6813}}) - As a result of an argument between the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests, the parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. * {{jalink|Culture|Q11042}} 1st year ({{jalink|1804|Q6894}}) - Constructed a water fountain using trees in the temple grounds {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}}2nd year ({{jalink|1819|Q6967}}) - Rebuild the Chozuya.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}.* Bunsei 12 ({{jalink|1819|Q6967}}) - Rebuild the worship hall, watariden, etc.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}18 ({{jalink|1733|Q6972}}) November 1st - {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} Lord {{jalink|Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachimangu Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}. * August 19, 4th year of {{jalink|Yasunaga}} ({{jalink|1775|Q7700}}) - {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} lord {{jalink|Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachiman Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=34}}. * {{jalink|Original|Q1145754}}2nd year ({{jalink|1737|Q6993}}) - Otabisho was built on the banks of the Ogi River{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}}6 ({{jalink|1859|Q7687}}) - Torii was rebuilt{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 28, {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}) - Promoted to {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Village company}}. The name of the shrine was changed from Hachiman-sha to Hachiman-gu {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}.* Meiji 13 ({{jalink|1880|Q7806}}) - Construction of the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. *October 26, 1907 ({{jalink|1907|Q2048}}) - Became a village shrine designated by Heihakuryokushinsha {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}6 ({{jalink|1931|Q18782}}) - Constructing the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * April 1934 ({{jalink|1934|Q18714}}) - Built a new inn on the banks of the Ogi River.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 1956 ({{jalink|1956|Q5221}}) - The former {{jalink|Narumi Town|Q11675375}} government building was relocated as a company office {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * 1969 ({{jalink|1959|Q5302}}) - Damaged by {{jalink|Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156}}, including the worship hall and water purification building.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * 1968 ({{jalink|1968|Q2644}}) - The worship hall, main hall, subsidiary shrine, and chozusha were newly built, and the name was changed to Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. * 1973 ({{jalink|1973|Q2477}}) - Transferred Suesha Inarisha from Aiden to Betsuden.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}* {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}12 ({{jalink|2000|Q1985}}) - Transferred the subsidiary shrine Kitano Tenmansha from the side hall to the separate hall{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}} === Main annual events === * Saitan Festival: New Year's Day {{jalink|New Year's Day|Q196627|New Year's Day}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Kitano Tenmansha {{jalink|Regular Festival|Q11385469}}: Early New Year {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Setsubun|Q746798}} Festival: February Setsubun Day {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Kigen Festival|Q11604982|Kigen Festival}}: February 11th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|prayer festival|Q118906710}}: Sunday in the second half of March {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Inari God|Q719665|Inari}} Festival: Sunday in May {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Ring ritual: July 30th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Korazusha Annual Festival: August 12th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Reitaisai (Omote Festival): Saturday in the first half of October {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387}}: Sunday in the first half of November {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Ninamesai|Q11501518}}: Around November 23rd{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Akibasha Annual Festival: December 16th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{jalink|Emperor's Birthday|Q72318|Tencho Festival}}: December 23rd {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}}* New Year's Eve Festival ({{jalink|Great Purification|Q11261910}}): December 31st {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Monthly Festival: First half of every month {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} === Precincts === === Sacred tree === It is a large camphor tree with a trunk 8 meters in diameter.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. It is said that it was already a large tree during the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}, and is estimated to be 1000 to 1200 years old. In 2000 ({{jalink|2000|Q1985}}), the Aichi Prefectural Arborist Association conducted treatment{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=23}}. === Office === It was built in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}36 ({{jalink|1903|Q2045}}) as the {{jalink|Narumi Town|Q11675375}} government building. {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}} It was moved to its current location as a shrine office in July of 1931 ({{jalink|1956|Q5221}}). It has been certified as a certified regional building asset by Nagoya City {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}. === Setsusha === Currently, there are the following ten subsidiary companies: {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=11}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}}. In the past, there were about five other companies. {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Current Sessuesha === * Inari Shrine: {{jalink|Uka no Mitama}} Okami{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=11}} * Korasha: {{jalink|Kenuchi Sukune}} Life{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kitano Tenmansha: {{jalink|Michizane Sugawara|Q382005}}Ko{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Akibasha: {{jalink|Kikunshuchi}} Life{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kokuzosha: {{jalink|Okuninushikami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Susanoosha: {{jalink|Susanoo}} Life{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kotohira Shrine: {{jalink|Oimono Nushikami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Okuwa Shrine: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}, {{jalink|Toyouke Omikami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Korazusha: {{jalink|Chikaji Onna no Mikoto|Q11596349|Chikkana no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Haraido Shrine: {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhime}} God, {{jalink|Kebukito Lord God|Q86734190}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Old Setsuesha === * Kitanosha: {{jalink|Takamisuhigami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Kokuzogu: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Doubunsha: {{jalink|Sarutabikojin}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} * Imori Shrine: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|Dainichikai Takayuki Spirit}}, {{jalink|Susanoo Mikoto||Susanoo Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Isagami}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Gallery === File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 02.jpg|Torii File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 04.jpg|Kitano Tenmansha File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 03.jpg|Korasusha === Festival === In the past, the festival was held throughout the town on the same day as {{jalink|Narumi Shrine|Q11495515}}, which is also located in Narumi, but in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink |1700|Q6813}}), a dispute arose between the two shrines, and since then the festival has been divided into front (Narumi Hachiman Shrine) and back (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=34}}. In recent years, there have been efforts to unite the front and back sides and gather floats in Narumi-juku Honmachi, and the festival is now being held as before the festival was divided.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} The Narumi Hachiman Shrine annual festival (Omote Festival) used to be held on August 15th, but since the first year of the Taisho era ({{jalink|1912|Q2077}}) it has been held on {{jalink|September 15th|Q2846}}, and since the Showa era it has been held on {{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}}. In recent years, the annual festival day has been set on a Saturday in the first half of {{jalink|October|Q124}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}. At the Shōjo Festival, a giant doll called {{jalink|shōjō}} made of a bamboo framework with a red face mask and upper half of the body covered with a costume appears, and there is the ``Narumi Hachiman Shrine Reitaisai Festival'' (Omote Festival), where adults wear shōjō dolls and chase children around. Shōto is the deity of Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. The monkeys hit children with fake hands and sticks, and it is said that children who are hit by the monkeys do not get sick.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes walk from {{jalink|Nagoya Railway|Q30850|Meitetsu}}{{jalink|Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|Q1137838|Nagoya Main Line}} {{jalink|Narumi Station|Q6966454}}. * About 20 minutes walk from {{jalink|Tokai Railway Company|Q513679|JR}}{{jalink|Tokaido Line (Nagoya area)|Q11235139|Tokaido Main Line}} {{jalink|Odaka Station|Q871424}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Source === Kawahara Shrine |Foundation = unknown (before {{ill|lt=13th century|Q7049|WD=Q7049}})|Map = {{Location map|Nagoya|lat_deg=35|lat_min=4|lat_sec=28.1|lon_deg=136|lon_min=56|lon_sec=36.3|label=Narumi Hachimangu Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|4|28.1|N|136|56|36.3|E}}}} }}'''Narumi Hachiman Shrine'' (Narumi Hachimangu) is {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Midori Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155209|WD=Q1155209|lt=Midori Ward}}{{jalink|Narumi Town {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=8}} located in (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Narumi Town}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} It is also said to be the Ronsha of ``{{ill|lt=Kawahara Shrine|Q3194327|WD=Q3194327}}'' and the ronsha of ``Izoe Shrine.'' === Overview === It has been called "Hachiman" since ancient times, and was considered to be a separate shrine to {{ill|lt=Narumi Shrine|Q11495515|WD=Q11495515}}. The location of the shrine was {{ill|lt=Edago|Q11533553|WD=Q11533553}} in {{ill||lt=Narumi Village|UNKNOWN}}, in the area that was called Zennoan Village in the {{ill||lt=Edo period|UNKNOWN}}. The year of its founding is unknown, but there are records of shrine occupations dating back to the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, and a {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722}} that is over 1,000 years old remains within the precincts, suggesting that it may be an old shrine that dates back to before the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}. As mentioned later, it is Shikinaisha's Ronsha. Around this shrine, the remains of a village from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858}}, ``Maenowa Ruins,'' have been discovered, and {{ill|lt=Yayoi pottery|Q11487461|WD=Q11487461}} have been unearthed. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Shinada Wake no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} (Senagatai Hibi no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayoribibimei}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Nishi Geishi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukiyomi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} === History === The company seems to have already existed in the Kamakura period, and there is a record that Motohisa Oga, the first generation member of the Kuno family, became a company official in the first year of {{ill|lt=Sadanaga|Q1194168|WD=Q1194168}} ({{ill|lt=1232|Q5418|WD=Q5418}}). Since ancient times, it has been called ``Hachimangu Shrine'' and was treated as a companion to Narumi Togu Shrine (Narumi Shrine). {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=During the Warring States period}}, there is a record that {{ill|lt=Yoshimoto Imagawa|Q1054305|WD=Q1054305}} and {{ill|lt=Shigemasa Yamaguchi|Q11467507|WD=Q11467507|lt=Chōjiro Shigemasa}} {{ill||lt=corporate territory|UNKNOWN}} was {{ill|lt=donated|Q11455717|WD=Q11455717}} to our company and Narumi Togu Shrine. According to ``{{ill||lt=Owari Makiyōki|UNKNOWN}},'' the priest Mr. Kuno submitted a document stating that Narumi Hachimangu Shrine is ``Kawahara Shrine,'' Aichi District, Owari Province, in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}.'' According to ``{{ill|lt=Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}'', there are some Edo-era fishing lanterns and {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} that have the words ``Kawahara Shrine'' written on them.On the other hand, there is a theory that it corresponds to "Izoe Shrine" in Aichi District, Owari Province, but according to Kunihiko Sakakibara, a {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Efn|{{ill||lt=local historian|UNKNOWN}}, Izoe Shrine was built during the Heian period. When it was abolished in the late period, Atsuta Daiguji disowned Hachimangu Shrine, and during the Oei era ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398}} to {{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315}}), the enshrinement site of Izoe Shrine, which had been abolished, was renamed to the old Jizoyama ({{jalink|Narumi Town) (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Q136913130|Q136913130|Komatsuyama, Narumi Town), it is said to have been rebuilt as a Hachimangu Shrine in the former Tange (Kiyomizu Temple, Narumi Town). It is said that in the early Muromachi period, it was moved from that place to the present-day Azazenowa area, which was at the mouth of the {{ill|lt=Ogigawa|Q11496839|WD=Q11496839|lt=Kurosuegawa}} large camphor tree. n|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|1984|pp=103}}. Izoe Shrine was later enshrined as Kitanosha, a subsidiary shrine of Hachimangu Shrine, but it is said to have been abolished in the Edo period {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}. }}, from the description in ``{{ill|lt=Owari Province Fudoki|Q135598023|WD=Q135598023}}'', it is presumed that Izoe Shrine was located in Aichi District {{ill|lt=Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture) #Go|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Hibego}}, and it is unreasonable to compare our company with Izoe Shrine.In {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}3 ({{ill|lt=1690|Q7678|WD=Q7678}}), he served as a priest at Narumi Shrine along with the Makino family.{{ill|lt=Narumi-juku|Q3033344|WD=Q3033344}}The Kuno family of Honmachi moved to Maenowa and served as a priest.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}13 ({{ill|lt=1700|Q6813|WD=Q6813}}), the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests quarreled and the festival was divided. The parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In recent years, efforts have been seen to bring together the front and back sides of the festival and gather floats at Narumi-juku Honmachi.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} === Chronology === * {{ill|lt=Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|WD=Q1143910|lt=Koji}} December 3, 17 ({{ill|lt=1557|Q6595|WD=Q6595}}) - Yoshimoto Imagawa donates Kanda 11 kan 50 mon to Hachiman Shrine and Narumi Togu (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 17th year ({{ill|lt=1589|Q6759|WD=Q6759}}) - Chojiro Shigemasa Yamaguchi donated 20 kanmon of shrine territory to the priests of both Hachimangu and Narumi Togu shrines.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} August 8 ({{ill|lt=1622|Q6815|WD=Q6815}}) - Reconstruction of the main shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}13 ({{ill|lt=1700|Q6813|WD=Q6813}}) - As a result of an argument between the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests, the parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. * {{ill|lt=Culture|Q11042|WD=Q11042}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1804|Q6894|WD=Q6894}}) - Constructed a water fountain using trees in the temple grounds {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}}2nd year ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967}}) - Rebuild the Chozuya.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}.* Bunsei 12 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967}}) - Rebuild the worship hall, watariden, etc.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}}18 ({{ill|lt=1733|Q6972|WD=Q6972}}) November 1st - {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} Lord {{ill|lt=Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871|WD=Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachimangu Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}. * August 19, 4th year of {{ill||lt=Yasunaga|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=1775|Q7700|WD=Q7700}}) - {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871|WD=Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachiman Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=34}}. * {{ill|lt=Original|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}}2nd year ({{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993}}) - Otabisho was built on the banks of the Ogi River{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}}6 ({{ill|lt=1859|Q7687|WD=Q7687}}) - Torii was rebuilt{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 28, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}) - Promoted to {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Village company}}. The name of the shrine was changed from Hachiman-sha to Hachiman-gu {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}.* Meiji 13 ({{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}}) - Construction of the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. *October 26, 1907 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}}) - Became a village shrine designated by Heihakuryokushinsha {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}6 ({{ill|lt=1931|Q18782|WD=Q18782}}) - Constructing the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * April 1934 ({{ill|lt=1934|Q18714|WD=Q18714}}) - Built a new inn on the banks of the Ogi River.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 1956 ({{ill|lt=1956|Q5221|WD=Q5221}}) - The former {{ill|lt=Narumi Town|Q11675375|WD=Q11675375}} government building was relocated as a company office {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * 1969 ({{ill|lt=1959|Q5302|WD=Q5302}}) - Damaged by {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}}, including the worship hall and water purification building.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * 1968 ({{ill|lt=1968|Q2644|WD=Q2644}}) - The worship hall, main hall, subsidiary shrine, and chozusha were newly built, and the name was changed to Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. * 1973 ({{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477}}) - Transferred Suesha Inarisha from Aiden to Betsuden.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}* {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}12 ({{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}}) - Transferred the subsidiary shrine Kitano Tenmansha from the side hall to the separate hall{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}} === Main annual events === * Saitan Festival: New Year's Day {{ill|lt=New Year's Day|Q196627|WD=Q196627|lt=New Year's Day}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Kitano Tenmansha {{ill|lt=Regular Festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}}: Early New Year {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798}} Festival: February Setsubun Day {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Kigen Festival|Q11604982|WD=Q11604982|lt=Kigen Festival}}: February 11th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=prayer festival|Q118906710|WD=Q118906710}}: Sunday in the second half of March {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Inari God|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} Festival: Sunday in May {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Ring ritual: July 30th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Korazusha Annual Festival: August 12th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Reitaisai (Omote Festival): Saturday in the first half of October {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}}: Sunday in the first half of November {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Ninamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}}: Around November 23rd{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Akibasha Annual Festival: December 16th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor's Birthday|Q72318|WD=Q72318|lt=Tencho Festival}}: December 23rd {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}}* New Year's Eve Festival ({{ill|lt=Great Purification|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}): December 31st {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Monthly Festival: First half of every month {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} === Precincts === === Sacred tree === It is a large camphor tree with a trunk 8 meters in diameter.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. It is said that it was already a large tree during the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845}}, and is estimated to be 1000 to 1200 years old. In 2000 ({{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}}), the Aichi Prefectural Arborist Association conducted treatment{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=23}}. === Office === It was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}36 ({{ill|lt=1903|Q2045|WD=Q2045}}) as the {{ill|lt=Narumi Town|Q11675375|WD=Q11675375}} government building. {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}} It was moved to its current location as a shrine office in July of 1931 ({{ill|lt=1956|Q5221|WD=Q5221}}). It has been certified as a certified regional building asset by Nagoya City {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}. === Setsusha === Currently, there are the following ten subsidiary companies: {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=11}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}}. In the past, there were about five other companies. {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Current Sessuesha === * Inari Shrine: {{ill||lt=Uka no Mitama|UNKNOWN}} Okami{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=11}} * Mikoto}}, {{ill||lt=Isagami|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Gallery === File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 02.jpg|Torii File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 04.jpg|Kitano Tenmansha File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 03.jpg|Korasusha === Festival === In the past, the festival was held throughout the town on the same day as {{ill|lt=Narumi Shrine|Q11495515|WD=Q11495515}}, which is also located in Narumi, but in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink |1700|Q6813}}), a dispute arose between the two shrines, and since then the festival has been divided into front (Narumi Hachiman Shrine) and back (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=34}}. In recent years, there have been efforts to unite the front and back sides and gather floats in Narumi-juku Honmachi, and the festival is now being held as before the festival was divided.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} The Narumi Hachiman Shrine annual festival (Omote Festival) used to be held on August 15th, but since the first year of the Taisho era ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|WD=Q2077}}) it has been held on {{ill|lt=September 15th|Q2846|WD=Q2846}}, and since the Showa era it has been held on {{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. In recent years, the annual festival day has been set on a Saturday in the first half of {{ill|lt=October|Q124|WD=Q124}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}. At the Shōjo Festival, a giant doll called {{ill||lt=shōjō|UNKNOWN}} made of a bamboo framework with a red face mask and upper half of the body covered with a costume appears, and there is the ``Narumi Hachiman Shrine Reitaisai Festival'' (Omote Festival), where adults wear shōjō dolls and chase children around. Shōto is the deity of Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. The monkeys hit children with fake hands and sticks, and it is said that children who are hit by the monkeys do not get sick.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes walk from {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railway|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu}}{{ill|lt=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|Q1137838|WD=Q1137838|lt=Nagoya Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Narumi Station|Q6966454|WD=Q6966454}}. * About 20 minutes walk from {{ill|lt=Tokai Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR}}{{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (Nagoya area)|Q11235139|WD=Q11235139|lt=Tokaido Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Odaka Narumi Shrine
Bettemiya
Kawahara Shrine |Foundation = unknown (before {{ill|lt=13th century|Q7049|WD=Q7049}})|Map = {{Location map|Nagoya|lat_deg=35|lat_min=4|lat_sec=28.1|lon_deg=136|lon_min=56|lon_sec=36.3|label=Narumi Hachimangu Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|4|28.1|N|136|56|36.3|E}}}} }}'''Narumi Hachiman Shrine'' (Narumi Hachimangu) is {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|WD=Q11751}}{{ill|lt=Midori Ward (Nagoya City)|Q1155209|WD=Q1155209|lt=Midori Ward}}{{jalink|Narumi Town {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=8}} located in (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Narumi Town}}. {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}{{ill|lt=Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Aichi District}} It is also said to be the Ronsha of ``{{ill|lt=Kawahara Shrine|Q3194327|WD=Q3194327}}'' and the ronsha of ``Izoe Shrine.'' === Overview === It has been called "Hachiman" since ancient times, and was considered to be a separate shrine to {{ill|lt=Narumi Shrine|Q11495515|WD=Q11495515}}. The location of the shrine was {{ill|lt=Edago|Q11533553|WD=Q11533553}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Narumi Village|UNKNOWN}}, in the area that was called Zennoan Village in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|UNKNOWN}}. The year of its founding is unknown, but there are records of shrine occupations dating back to the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, and a {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722}} that is over 1,000 years old remains within the precincts, suggesting that it may be an old shrine that dates back to before the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}. As mentioned later, it is Shikinaisha's Ronsha. Around this shrine, the remains of a village from the {{ill|lt=Yayoi period|Q207858|WD=Q207858}}, ``Maenowa Ruins,'' have been discovered, and {{ill|lt=Yayoi pottery|Q11487461|WD=Q11487461}} have been unearthed. === Enshrined God === * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} (Shinada Wake no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} (Senagatai Hibi no Mikoto) {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayoribibimei}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Nishi Geishi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} * {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukiyomi}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=3}} === History === The company seems to have already existed in the Kamakura period, and there is a record that Motohisa Oga, the first generation member of the Kuno family, became a company official in the first year of {{ill|lt=Sadanaga|Q1194168|WD=Q1194168}} ({{ill|lt=1232|Q5418|WD=Q5418}}). Since ancient times, it has been called ``Hachimangu Shrine'' and was treated as a companion to Narumi Togu Shrine (Narumi Shrine). {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=During the Warring States period}}, there is a record that {{ill|lt=Yoshimoto Imagawa|Q1054305|WD=Q1054305}} and {{ill|lt=Shigemasa Yamaguchi|Q11467507|WD=Q11467507|lt=Chōjiro Shigemasa}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=corporate territory|UNKNOWN}} was {{ill|lt=donated|Q11455717|WD=Q11455717}} to our company and Narumi Togu Shrine. According to ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Owari Makiyōki|UNKNOWN}},'' the priest Mr. Kuno submitted a document stating that Narumi Hachimangu Shrine is ``Kawahara Shrine,'' Aichi District, Owari Province, in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}.'' According to ``{{ill|lt=Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}'', there are some Edo-era fishing lanterns and {{ill|lt=ridge tags|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} that have the words ``Kawahara Shrine'' written on them.On the other hand, there is a theory that it corresponds to "Izoe Shrine" in Aichi District, Owari Province, but according to Kunihiko Sakakibara, a {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Efn|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=local historian|UNKNOWN}}, Izoe Shrine was built during the Heian period. When it was abolished in the late period, Atsuta Daiguji disowned Hachimangu Shrine, and during the Oei era ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|WD=Q6398}} to {{ill|lt=1428|Q6315|WD=Q6315}}), the enshrinement site of Izoe Shrine, which had been abolished, was renamed to the old Jizoyama ({{jalink|Narumi Town) (Nagoya City)|Q136913130|Q136913130|Q136913130|Komatsuyama, Narumi Town), it is said to have been rebuilt as a Hachimangu Shrine in the former Tange (Kiyomizu Temple, Narumi Town). It is said that in the early Muromachi period, it was moved from that place to the present-day Azazenowa area, which was at the mouth of the {{ill|lt=Ogigawa|Q11496839|WD=Q11496839|lt=Kurosuegawa}} large camphor tree. n|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2020|pp=64}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|1984|pp=103}}. Izoe Shrine was later enshrined as Kitanosha, a subsidiary shrine of Hachimangu Shrine, but it is said to have been abolished in the Edo period {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=5}}. }}, from the description in ``{{ill|lt=Owari Province Fudoki|Q135598023|WD=Q135598023}}'', it is presumed that Izoe Shrine was located in Aichi District {{ill|lt=Aichi County (Aichi Prefecture) #Go|Q552687|WD=Q552687|lt=Hibego}}, and it is unreasonable to compare our company with Izoe Shrine.In {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}3 ({{ill|lt=1690|Q7678|WD=Q7678}}), he served as a priest at Narumi Shrine along with the Makino family.{{ill|lt=Narumi-juku|Q3033344|WD=Q3033344}}The Kuno family of Honmachi moved to Maenowa and served as a priest.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}13 ({{ill|lt=1700|Q6813|WD=Q6813}}), the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests quarreled and the festival was divided. The parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. In recent years, efforts have been seen to bring together the front and back sides of the festival and gather floats at Narumi-juku Honmachi.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} === Chronology === * {{ill|lt=Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|WD=Q1143910|lt=Koji}} December 3, 17 ({{ill|lt=1557|Q6595|WD=Q6595}}) - Yoshimoto Imagawa donates Kanda 11 kan 50 mon to Hachiman Shrine and Narumi Togu (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 17th year ({{ill|lt=1589|Q6759|WD=Q6759}}) - Chojiro Shigemasa Yamaguchi donated 20 kanmon of shrine territory to the priests of both Hachimangu and Narumi Togu shrines.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. * {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}} August 8 ({{ill|lt=1622|Q6815|WD=Q6815}}) - Reconstruction of the main shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}13 ({{ill|lt=1700|Q6813|WD=Q6813}}) - As a result of an argument between the Makino family priests and the Kuno family priests, the parishioners from Sakumachi to Nakajima changed from those of Narumi Shrine to those of Hachimangu Shrine, and the festival began to be held in front and behind the scenes {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=48}}. * {{ill|lt=Culture|Q11042|WD=Q11042}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1804|Q6894|WD=Q6894}}) - Constructed a water fountain using trees in the temple grounds {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}}2nd year ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967}}) - Rebuild the Chozuya.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}.* Bunsei 12 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967}}) - Rebuild the worship hall, watariden, etc.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}}18 ({{ill|lt=1733|Q6972|WD=Q6972}}) November 1st - {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} Lord {{ill|lt=Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871|WD=Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachimangu Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=88}}. * August 19, 4th year of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=1775|Q7700|WD=Q7700}}) - {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} lord {{ill|lt=Muneharu Tokugawa|Q6636871|WD=Q6636871}} visits Narumi Hachiman Shrine {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=34}}. * {{ill|lt=Original|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754}}2nd year ({{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993}}) - Otabisho was built on the banks of the Ogi River{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}}6 ({{ill|lt=1859|Q7687|WD=Q7687}}) - Torii was rebuilt{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 28, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}) - Promoted to {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Village company}}. The name of the shrine was changed from Hachiman-sha to Hachiman-gu {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}.* Meiji 13 ({{ill|lt=1880|Q7806|WD=Q7806}}) - Construction of the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. *October 26, 1907 ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}}) - Became a village shrine designated by Heihakuryokushinsha {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=4}}. * {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}6 ({{ill|lt=1931|Q18782|WD=Q18782}}) - Constructing the shrine building {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * April 1934 ({{ill|lt=1934|Q18714|WD=Q18714}}) - Built a new inn on the banks of the Ogi River.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * July 1956 ({{ill|lt=1956|Q5221|WD=Q5221}}) - The former {{ill|lt=Narumi Town|Q11675375|WD=Q11675375}} government building was relocated as a company office {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}. * 1969 ({{ill|lt=1959|Q5302|WD=Q5302}}) - Damaged by {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}}, including the worship hall and water purification building.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=15}}. * 1968 ({{ill|lt=1968|Q2644|WD=Q2644}}) - The worship hall, main hall, subsidiary shrine, and chozusha were newly built, and the name was changed to Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. * 1973 ({{ill|lt=1973|Q2477|WD=Q2477}}) - Transferred Suesha Inarisha from Aiden to Betsuden.{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}* {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}12 ({{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}}) - Transferred the subsidiary shrine Kitano Tenmansha from the side hall to the separate hall{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}} === Main annual events === * Saitan Festival: New Year's Day {{ill|lt=New Year's Day|Q196627|WD=Q196627|lt=New Year's Day}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Kitano Tenmansha {{ill|lt=Regular Festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}}: Early New Year {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798}} Festival: February Setsubun Day {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Kigen Festival|Q11604982|WD=Q11604982|lt=Kigen Festival}}: February 11th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=prayer festival|Q118906710|WD=Q118906710}}: Sunday in the second half of March {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Inari God|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} Festival: Sunday in May {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Ring ritual: July 30th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Korazusha Annual Festival: August 12th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Reitaisai (Omote Festival): Saturday in the first half of October {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}}: Sunday in the first half of November {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Ninamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}}: Around November 23rd{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Akibasha Annual Festival: December 16th {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor's Birthday|Q72318|WD=Q72318|lt=Tencho Festival}}: December 23rd {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}}* New Year's Eve Festival ({{ill|lt=Great Purification|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}): December 31st {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} * Monthly Festival: First half of every month {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=25}} === Precincts === === Sacred tree === It is a large camphor tree with a trunk 8 meters in diameter.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=47}}. It is said that it was already a large tree during the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845}}, and is estimated to be 1000 to 1200 years old. In 2000 ({{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|WD=Q1985}}), the Aichi Prefectural Arborist Association conducted treatment{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=23}}. === Office === It was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}36 ({{ill|lt=1903|Q2045|WD=Q2045}}) as the {{ill|lt=Narumi Town|Q11675375|WD=Q11675375}} government building. {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=14}} It was moved to its current location as a shrine office in July of 1931 ({{ill|lt=1956|Q5221|WD=Q5221}}). It has been certified as a certified regional building asset by Nagoya City {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2000|pp=89}}. === Setsusha === Currently, there are the following ten subsidiary companies: {{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=11}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}}. Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Dainichikai Takayuki Spirit}}, {{ill|lt=Susanoo Mikoto||WD=|lt=Susanoo Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Isagami|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2004|pp=12}} === Gallery === File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 02.jpg|Torii File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 04.jpg|Kitano Tenmansha File:Narumi Yahata Shrine 03.jpg|Korasusha === Festival === In the past, the festival was held throughout the town on the same day as {{ill|lt=Narumi Shrine|Q11495515|WD=Q11495515}}, which is also located in Narumi, but in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}13 ({{jalink |1700|Q6813}}), a dispute arose between the two shrines, and since then the festival has been divided into front (Narumi Hachiman Shrine) and back (Narumi Shrine) {{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=34}}. In recent years, there have been efforts to unite the front and back sides and gather floats in Narumi-juku Honmachi, and the festival is now being held as before the festival was divided.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}} The Narumi Hachiman Shrine annual festival (Omote Festival) used to be held on August 15th, but since the first year of the Taisho era ({{ill|lt=1912|Q2077|WD=Q2077}}) it has been held on {{ill|lt=September 15th|Q2846|WD=Q2846}}, and since the Showa era it has been held on {{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}. In recent years, the annual festival day has been set on a Saturday in the first half of {{ill|lt=October|Q124|WD=Q124}}{{Sfn|Sakakibara|2005|pp=26}}. At the Shōjo Festival, a giant doll called {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shōjō|UNKNOWN}} made of a bamboo framework with a red face mask and upper half of the body covered with a costume appears, and there is the ``Narumi Hachiman Shrine Reitaisai Festival'' (Omote Festival), where adults wear shōjō dolls and chase children around. Shōto is the deity of Narumi Hachimangu Shrine. The monkeys hit children with fake hands and sticks, and it is said that children who are hit by the monkeys do not get sick.{{Sfn|Awakawa|2013|pp=35}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes walk from {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railway|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu}}{{ill|lt=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|Q1137838|WD=Q1137838|lt=Nagoya Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Narumi Station|Q6966454|WD=Q6966454}}. * About 20 minutes walk from {{ill|lt=Tokai Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR}}{{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (Nagoya area)|Q11235139|WD=Q11235139|lt=Tokaido Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Odaka Station|Q871424|WD=Q871424}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Notelist}} === Source === {{Reflist}} === References === * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=Kunihiko
